nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; found around the Ryukyu Islands and Bonin Islands in subtropical marine environments

diet.notes: Primarily planktivorous in the wild; generally accepts frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and other meaty zooplankton preparations in captivity. Has been documented also consuming small amounts of filamentous algae. Tends to do best with frequent small feedings (2–3 times daily) rather than single large meals.

summary: A smaller anthias from subtropical Japanese reefs; generally kept in groups of one male and several females. Known for its protogynous sex-change behavior and need for frequent feeding.
careNotes:
Pink Anthias (*Pseudanthias taira*) is a reef-associated species endemic to the subtropical Northwest Pacific, most commonly reported from the Ryukyu Islands and Bonin Islands of Japan. It is a protogynous hermaphrodite — females can transition to male if the dominant male is lost. Groups are best kept as one male with multiple females; keeping multiple males together tends to result in significant aggression. Like most anthias, this species has been observed to benefit from frequent small feedings (2–3× daily) to maintain body condition. It is generally considered reef-safe and tends to coexist with corals and most peaceful invertebrates without issue. The species is not commonly available in the hobby and may require some patience to source; newly acquired specimens can sometimes take several days to begin accepting frozen foods, so targeted feeding with live or freshly thawed foods initially is advisable. Note: LiveAquaria's listing for this species describes a range spanning the Red Sea and Great Barrier Reef, which appears to be generic anthias care text and is not confirmed by FishBase, which restricts *P. taira* to the Northwest Pacific. The family placement has also been updated — modern taxonomy places it in Anthiadidae rather than Serranidae. FishBase lists the species as Data Deficient (IUCN DD, 2022), reflecting limited biological documentation.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The flagged concern about LiveAquaria's boilerplate range (Red Sea / Great Barrier Reef) is confirmed spurious. FishBase country list (accessed 2026-06-03) lists exactly three native territories: Japan, Ryukyu Islands, and Bonin Islands — all Northwest Pacific. The nativeRange fields in this record already reflect the correct restricted distribution; no change to geographic data was required. Additionally confirmed via FishBase *Pseudanthias squamipinnis* ecology notes (type species for genus): all *Pseudanthias* are midwater planktivorous constant grazers (zooplankton feeders that feed continuously throughout the day), haremic, and protogynous — consistent with the 2–3× daily feeding guidance already in this record. fish.constantGrazer updated from null to true; fish.sexChange and fish.haremic were already correctly set.
breedingNotes: Pseudanthias taira is a protogynous sequential hermaphrodite. If the dominant male in a group is removed or dies, the largest, most dominant female will typically transition to male over a period of days to weeks. Successful captive breeding has not been well-documented for this species; aquarium spawning of anthias in general is considered advanced.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (family Anthiadidae), adult size (max 10 cm TL), and native range. FishBase restricts the range to Northwest Pacific: Japan (Ryukyu Islands, Bonin Islands), contradicting LiveAquaria's broader Indo-Pacific/Red Sea description. Adult size averaged: LA ~12.7 cm vs FishBase 10 cm TL → used 6–11 cm range. Family updated to Anthiadidae per current classification.

additional.name: FishBase Country List
additional.notes: Confirmed native range: Japan, Bonin Islands, Ryukyu Islands only (3 territories, all Northwest Pacific).

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; sex change from female to male occurs if dominant male is lost. Captive breeding rarely documented.
